title: Micah
author: Micah of Moresheth, a rural Judean prophet contemporary with Isaiah
date: ~735–700 BC (during Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah's reigns)
setting: Judah and Samaria on the eve of the Assyrian crisis. Micah was a rural prophet — his perspective contrasts with the urban elite whom he condemns.
purpose: To pronounce judgment on both Israel and Judah for injustice, false prophecy, and corrupt leadership, and to announce the coming messianic king born in Bethlehem who will shepherd the flock.
themes: God's case against corrupt leadership and false prophets
Social justice: land theft, exploitation of the poor
Judgment on both Israel and Judah
The messianic king born in Bethlehem (5:2)
'What does the LORD require of you?' (6:8)

outline.label: Promise of Restoration and the Messianic King
God's Lawsuit against Israel and Hope

before.label: Isaiah — contemporaneous preaching in Jerusalem's courts
Rural poor exploited by Jerusalem's urban elites
Fall of Samaria — the northern kingdom gone

after.label: Hezekiah's reforms — Micah cited as their catalyst
Jesus born in Bethlehem — Micah 5:2 fulfilled
Micah 6:8 — 'do justice, love kindness, walk humbly'

key_people.name: Micah of Moresheth
key_people.role: Rural prophet from the Shephelah of Judah, contemporary with Isaiah (c. 735–700 BC); his outsider perspective enables him to speak with devastating frankness to both Jerusalem's leaders and the rural poor they exploit

context: Micah prophesied during the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah — a period that saw the fall of the northern kingdom to Assyria (722 BC) and the Assyrian siege of Jerusalem under Sennacherib (701 BC). His message addresses both the economic exploitation of the rural poor by Jerusalem's landowners and the false security provided by the city's religious establishment. He is quoted in Jeremiah 26:18 as having influenced Hezekiah's repentance.
christ_connection: Micah 5:2 is one of the most precise messianic prophecies in the OT — that the ruler of Israel would come from Bethlehem Ephrathah, whose origins are 'from of old, from ancient days.' Matthew and Luke both cite this as fulfilled in Jesus' birth in Bethlehem (Matt 2:6; Luke 2:4–7), and it is this passage that the chief priests and scribes cite when Herod asks where the Christ is to be born. Micah 6:8 ('do justice, love kindness, walk humbly with your God') is the OT's most compact summary of the life that the gospel produces, summarizing the law's demand in terms that Jesus himself embodies.

key_verses.note: 'But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah... from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days' — fulfilled in Jesus' birth (Matt 2:6)

'He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God?' — the most concentrated OT summary of covenant obligation

themes_detail.title: Economic Justice and the Prophetic Indictment
themes_detail.text: Micah's indictment of the powerful (2:1–2; 3:1–4; 6:10–12) is precise and concrete: they covet fields and seize them, defraud with false weights and measures, and use their positions to exploit those with no recourse. This is not abstract moralizing but a description of the economic structure of 8th-century Judah, where urban elites were consolidating the small family holdings that were the intended basis of Israelite society. Micah insists that economic exploitation is a covenant violation with the same seriousness as idolatry.

themes_detail.title: False Prophets and Authentic Prophecy
themes_detail.text: Micah 3:5–8 contains one of the OT's sharpest critiques of the prophetic establishment: prophets who cry 'peace' when paid and declare war when not given food are telling people what they want to hear. Micah contrasts their performance with his own calling: 'I am filled with power, with the Spirit of the LORD, and with justice and might, to declare to Jacob his transgression and to Israel his sin' (3:8). The mark of authentic prophecy, in Micah's account, is not popularity but willingness to confront sin at personal cost.

themes_detail.title: Jerusalem's Judgment and Future Glory
themes_detail.text: Micah oscillates between threat and promise in a way that can confuse readers expecting linear progression. Jerusalem will be plowed as a field (3:12 — the verse Jeremiah quotes); yet the mountain of the LORD will be established as the highest of mountains and nations will stream to it (4:1–3, nearly identical to Isaiah 2:2–4). These are not contradictions but eschatological tensions: judgment falls on the corrupt city, but through judgment God brings about a purified and glorified Zion. The cruciform pattern — death and resurrection — is the shape of Micah's hope.

themes_detail.title: The Covenant Lawsuit and God's Character
themes_detail.text: Micah 6:1–8 is structured as a legal proceeding: God calls the mountains and hills as witnesses to a lawsuit against his people. The exchange clarifies what the covenant is: not a system of sacrifices (the people ask what offerings would be sufficient) but a relationship of justice, kindness, and humility. God's response identifies the genuine requirements — do justice, love kindness, walk humbly — not as new demands but as the relational essence that ritual was always meant to express and form. This lawsuit form anticipates the NT's insight that the law's deepest intent is love.
